content.full_text:
/
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
August11, 1997
Douglas M. Grandy
Office of Energy Assessments
717 “K” Street, Suite 409
Sacramento, California 95814
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-361
Dear Mr. Grandy:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTION
As Program Manager of Energy Assessments in the Department of General Services, may
you accept compensation for your services as a governor on the newly created California Power
Exchange Corporation?
CONCLUSION
You may accept compensation for your services as a governor because such payments are
not prohibited by the honoraria provisions of the Act. Compensation from the corporation may
be a disqualifying source of income to you unless it is considered government salary. You may
not represent CPEC for compensation before the Department of General Services for the purpose
of influencing action on a contract, grant, loan, license, permit or other entitlement for use.
FACTS
You are the Program Manager of Energy Assessments in the Department of General
Services. In that capacity, you are responsible for developing and implementing policy for state
and local government agencies with a goal toward achieving more efficient and cost effective
energy usage and management for those agencies. Over the years, you have developed
considerable background and expertise in public sector energy issues and policy.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
1 MEHTA
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENT, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. A-97-361
Page 2
You were recently appointed to serve as a governor of the newly created California
Power Exchange Corporation (CPEC). As part of last year’s comprehensive statutory
restructuring of California’s electricity industry, AB 1890 (Chapter 854, Statutes of 1996)
authorized the creation of the CPEC, a nonprofit public benefit corporation.
Your appointment as a governor results from your work with the State of California.
However, you serve as a governor in an individual capacity and not as a representative of the
Department of General Services or the State of California. Section 17 ofthe corporation’s
bylaws provides that as a governor, you shall receive compensation in the amount of $300 for
attendance at each meeting of the governing board or committee ofthe governing board. You
will be compensated in the amount of $150 for participation in meetings by telephone.
Additionally, you are entitled to receive reimbursement for reasonable and necessary travel and
other actual expenses incurred in performing your duties and in attending meetings.
ANALYSIS
1. Honoraria
Section 89502(c) provides that no designated employee of a state agency shall accept an
honorarium from any source ifthe employee would be required to report the receipt of income or
gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic interests.
The term “honorarium” is defined in section 89501(a) as any payment made in
consideration for any speech given, article published, or attendance at any public or private
conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal or like gathering. Regulation 18931.3
defines “attendance” as being present during, making an appearance at, or serving as host or
master of ceremonies for, any public or private conference, convention, meeting, social event,
meal or like gathering.
You serve as a governor of CPEC in an individual capacity. The corporation’s bylaws
provide that as a governor you will receive compensation in the amount of $300 for attendance at
each meeting of the governing board or committee of the governing board. Additionally, you
will receive $150 for participation in meetings by telephone and you will receive reimbursement
for travel and other necessary expenses incurred in performing your duties and in attending
meetings. Such payments are not in consideration for attending meetings. Rather, the
compensation you receive as a governor is in the nature ofper diem or salary because it is in
exchange for ongoing substantive services. (Glaser Advice Letter, No. 96-045.) Accordingly,
the honorarium prohibition does not apply to your circumstances.


File No. A-97-361
Page 3
2. Conflict of Interest
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
malcing, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on any source of income of $250 or more to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) The
corporation bylaws provide that you will receive $300 for each meeting that you attend as a
governor. Accordingly, receipt of this income may subject you to the disqualification provisions
of the Act. As Program Manager of Energy Assessments in the Department of General Services,
you may be disqualified from making, participating in making, or attempting to use your official
position to influence a governmental decision ifthe decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on CPEC.
You will not be subject to disqualification ifyour income from CPEC is considered
government salary. Income, for purposes ofthe conflict of interest provisions ofthe Act does not
include salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state government
agency. (Section 82030(b)(2).) CPEC may qualify as a state government agency if certain
criteria are met as set forth in In re Siegel (3 FPPC Ops. 62):
1) Whether the impetus for formation ofthe corporation originated
with a government agency;
2) Whether it is substantially funded by, or its primary source of
funds is, a government agency;
3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which it is formed is
to provide services or undertake obligations which public agencies
are legally authorized to perform and which, in fact, they
traditionally have performed; and
4) Whether the corporation is treated as a public entity by other
statutory provisions,
It is likely that CPEC is a government agency under the Siegel opinion. If CPEC
qualifies as a government agency, compensation from CPEC will not be a source of disqualifying
income for you.


File No. A-97-361
Page 4
3. Section 87104
Section 87104 prohibits any public official of a state agency from acting as an agent or
attorney for, or otherwise representing, any other person for compensation before his or her
agency or any officer or employee thereof, if the appearance or communication is made for
the purpose of influencing action on a contract, grant, loan, license, permit or other entitlement
for use.
Section 87104 was recently amended by the Legislature in AB 937 (Chapter 145, Statutes
of 1998). Under the old law, you would not be permitted to represent CPEC before the
Department of General Services if the appearance or communication is made for the purpose of
influencing action on a contract, grant, loan, license, permit or other entitlement for use. The
new law exempts from this prohibition an employee of a state agency when the public official is
representing his or her employing agency. Thus, under the new law and assuming that CPEC
qualifies as a state governmental agency, you are permitted to represent CPEC before the
Department of General Services after the new law goes into effect. The new law will go into
effect January 1, 1998.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
-
By:
Julia Butcher
Graduate Legal Assistant, Legal Division
SGC:JB:jw
